macro_segments.title: Vows Preventing a Wife from Visiting Her Father's House: Conditions for Tolerability

micro_segments.title: Introduction to the Mishnah: A Man's Vow Preventing His Wife from Going to Her Father's House
Cases Where the Parents Live in the Same City: One Month Tolerable, Beyond That Requires Divorce
Cases Where Parents Live in a Different City: One Pilgrimage Sustainable, Three Requires Divorce
Gemara's Internal Contradiction: Reconciling One, Two, and Three Pilgrimage Periods
Abaye's Resolution: Distinguishing Between a Woman Prone to Visiting and One Who Visits Occasionally
Midrashic Interlude: The Bride Finding Favor in Her Husband's House and Remaining Connected to Her Parents

macro_segments.title: The Nature of Marriage: From Master-Servant to Equal Partnership (Ish and Isha)

micro_segments.title: Prophetic Vision of Restored Marriage: Calling Husband 'Ish' Rather Than 'Ba'al'
Linguistic and Conceptual Differences: Ba'al as Master and Ish as Equal Partner
Contemporary Feminist Perspectives: Ish and Isha as Expressing Equal Partnership
Rashi's Explanation: The Bride at Peace in Her Husband's House and the Metaphor of Union

macro_segments.title: Vows Preventing Attendance at Weddings and Funerals: The Communal Dimension

micro_segments.title: Mishnah: A Vow Preventing Attendance at Weddings or Mourning Houses Requires Divorce Without Ketubah
Gemara's Question: Why Is Preventing Mourning House Attendance Considered 'Locking the Door'?
Braita's Answer: Preventing Mourning Attendance Damages Reciprocal Social Obligations
Rabbi Meir's Teaching: The Value of Attending Funerals and Communal Responsibility
Consequences of Avoiding Funerals: No One Will Eulogize, Bury, or Accompany Her

macro_segments.title: The Legitimate Exception: Vows May Be Permitted If Based on Valid Concerns About Dangerous Environments

micro_segments.title: Mishnah's Exception Clause: If He Has Another Legitimate Reason, the Vow Is Permissible
Rav Yehudah and Shmuel's Reason: Vow Justified by Presence of Promiscuous People
Rav Ashi's Requirement: The Danger Must Be Established, Not Merely Claimed

macro_segments.title: Vows Imposing Silence and Impossible Demands: Revealing Secrets and Absurd Labor

micro_segments.title: First Case: A Vow Forcing the Wife to Reveal Private Conversations with Her Husband
Rav Yehudah and Shmuel's Explanation: Such Matters Are Shameful and Should Remain Private
Second Case: A Vow Forcing Meaningless Labor (Filling and Spilling into the Dunghill)
Shmuel's Interpretation: The Vow Prevents Pregnancy by Forcing the Wife to Wash Out Semen
Rabbah bar bar Chanah's Alternative: The Issue Is That She Appears Foolish and Crazy
Connection to Domestic Control: Absurd Demands Reflect Broader Marital Abuse

macro_segments.title: Vows Preventing Borrowing and Lending: Social Standing in the Community

micro_segments.title: Rav Kahana's Case: A Vow Preventing the Wife from Borrowing or Lending Vessels
Reason for Divorce: The Vow Gives Her a Bad Reputation Among Her Neighbors
Reciprocal Case: A Wife's Own Vow Against Borrowing Also Creates Bad Reputation
Extended Case: Vows Against Weaving Nice Clothes for Stepchildren Also Create Bad Reputation for Husband
Broader Principle: Community Standing Affects Both Wife and Husband Through Shared Social Relations

macro_segments.title: Women Who Leave Without Ketubah: Those Who Transgress the Law of Moshe

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Introduction: Women Who Transgress the Law of Moshe and the Religion of Jewish Women
First Category: Feeding Husband Food Without Tithes (Ma'aserot and Terumot)
Gemara's Question: How Can He Claim She Fed Him Untithed Food If He Knew?
Gemara's Answer: Evidence Can Emerge Indirectly (Via Third Parties)
Second Category: Having Sexual Relations While in a State of Ritual Impurity (Niddah)
Reliance on Wife's Counting: A Man May Rely on His Wife's Determination of Purity
Third Category: Failing to Separate Challah from the Dough
Fourth Category: Making Vows and Not Fulfilling Them
The Theological Consequence: Unfulfilled Vows Result in the Death of Children
Rabbi Meir's Approach: The Husband Should Provoke Vows to Be Able to Annul Them
Rejection of Meir's Approach: 'One Cannot Dwell with a Snake in a Basket'

macro_segments.title: Women Who Leave Without Ketubah: Those Who Transgress the Religion of Jewish Women

micro_segments.title: Mishnah Introduction: Four Examples of Transgressing Jewish Women's Practices
First Example: Going Out in Public with Uncovered Head (Rosh Parua)
Biblical Source: The Sotah Ritual and the Covering of the Head
Rashi's Interpretation: Head Covering as a Norm Rather Than a Biblical Prohibition
Gemara's Distinction: Biblical Law Requires Minimal Cover; Jewish Practice Requires More
Rabbi Zeira's Question: In What Context Is the Minimal Covering Sufficient?
Abaye's Answer: Minimal Cover Needed When Traveling Between Courtyards via Alley
The Physical Layout: Courtyards, Alleys, and Marketplace as Zones of Privacy
The Focus on Public Space: Covering Requirements Tied to Visibility to Strangers
Second Example: Knitting in the Marketplace and Exposing Arms
Rav Chisda's Interpretation: Red Thread or Suggestive Positioning in Knitting
Third Example: Speaking Familiarly with All Men (Flirtation)
Rav Yehudah and Shmuel's Interpretation: Joking Around with Young Men
Anecdote: The Arab Woman and the Spindle as Example of Improper Flirtation
Abba Shaul's Addition: Cursing Her Husband's Parents in His Presence
Fourth Example: Being Excessively Loud (the Kolanit)
Rav Yehudah and Shmuel's Interpretation: Loud During Sexual Relations
Rashi's Explanation: Either Pain or Pleasure Expressed Loudly
Gemara's Question: Why List the Kolanit Here Rather Than Among Physical Defects?
Gemara's Answer: Loudness Is Controllable Behavior, Not an Intrinsic Defect
